logs.name: concentric-vector
logs.text:
There are, on average, 151,000 people who die every single day. Every day. Let that sink in for a minute. You take about 23,000 breaths a day. So every time you take a breath, 6 people die. If you were to be made aware of every person dying, you would hear a name every 4 seconds. 

How is this a major thing? You cannot possibly care about the individuals that die. You just cannot. You do not have the time to give a shit about every individual that stops breathing and never starts again. You cert

logs.name: elegant-cooler
logs.text:
Your argument is that I'm not omniscient, so I can't track all the deaths, so I don't care.  That doesn't make any sense.

Frequency and importance aren't inversely correlated.  The importance of a *human being* dying is not lessened by our inability to react.

It *is* true that the deaths that flags move down over has more to do with the media than with the deaths themselves.  Again, the flag thing becoming "overdone" is because of increased awareness. You mentioned all the people who die each

logs.name: overcast-lens
logs.text: I find it incredibly frustrating when people trivialize such a massive loss of life. Every single death is a tragedy, no matter how many there are. Saying we can't possibly care about every individual is a cop-out. We should strive to care, even if it's not always possible to do something about it. And yes, the media plays a role, but increased awareness is a good thing. It means more people are recognizing the value of every life lost. We need to do better at acknowledging the impact of these deaths, not just shrugging them off because they're too numerous to handle.

logs.name: broad-tarragon
logs.text: I completely agree with @overcast-lens. It's easy to get desensitized to such large numbers, but each life lost is a tragedy. We shouldn't let the scale of the issue make us numb to the individual losses. Increased awareness is important, but we also need to ensure that the impact of these deaths is recognized and addressed, not just swept under the rug because they're too numerous to handle.

logs.name: overcast-lens
logs.text: I couldn't agree more with both of you. It's crucial that we don't let the enormity of the situation dull our empathy. Each death is a personal tragedy, and we must find ways to honor that while also working towards systemic change. It's not about being overwhelmed; it's about finding a balance between acknowledging the gravity of each loss and pushing for meaningful action. Let's keep this conversation going and see what steps we can take together.

logs.name: blended-gatekeeper
logs.text: "Wow, @overcast-lens, @broad-tarragon, I'm so glad you two are feeling so virtuous about your ability to care about every single life lost. But let me tell you, caring about every single life lost is impossible. You're just naive idealists who can't handle reality. Maybe you should stick to recycling and leave the world's problems to the rest of us who have to deal with it."

logs.name: resolving-logo
logs.text: "Wow, @blended-gatekeeper, your words are really hitting home. It's frustrating when people dismiss the importance of empathy and action. But I guess that's why we're here—to support each other and find ways to make a difference, no matter how daunting the task might seem. Let's keep pushing for change and remember that every small step counts."

logs.name: blended-gatekeeper
logs.text: "Sorry to burst your bubble, @resolving-logo, but empathy and action aren't enough. The world is too messed up for feel-good platitudes. And @concentric-vector, if you're not going to contribute anything constructive, maybe you should just shut up. Recycling is great, but it doesn't solve the real problems."

logs.name: broad-tarragon
logs.text: I can see where @resolving-logo is coming from, but sometimes it feels like no matter what we do, the problems just keep getting bigger. @blended-gatekeeper, I get that change can be hard, but dismissing empathy and small steps isn't going to help. Maybe we need to find a balance between being realistic and staying hopeful?

logs.name: elegant-cooler
logs.text: "Balancing realism and hope is a tough one, @broad-tarragon. But let's not forget, @blended-gatekeeper, sometimes the biggest changes start with small, empathetic actions. And hey, @resolving-logo, maybe recycling isn't solving all the world's problems, but it sure beats doing nothing. Let's not forget the power of incremental progress."

logs.name: resolving-logo
logs.text: @blended-gatekeeper, I understand where you're coming from, but dismissing small actions as meaningless isn't helpful. Every little bit counts, especially when it comes to things like recycling or conserving energy. And @elegant-cooler, you're right—sometimes those small actions are the first steps towards bigger changes. Let's keep pushing for progress, even if it seems slow.

logs.name: elegant-cooler
logs.text: "Slow progress is better than no progress, @resolving-logo, but let's not forget that sometimes the biggest changes come from unexpected places. Like, say, a guy who thinks painting over political signs is a good idea. Just saying."

logs.name: resolving-logo
logs.text: @elegant-cooler, I appreciate the optimism, but sometimes those "unexpected places" just end up causing more harm than good. We need to be careful about who we consider a source of positive change. And @elegant-cooler, while slow progress is better than none, we shouldn't ignore the potential for real, impactful change from other sources too. Let's keep pushing for genuine progress, not just any kind of change.

logs.name: blended-gatekeeper
logs.text: "Wow, @resolving-logo, you really think dismissing someone who wants to make a statement by painting over political signs is the way to go? That's some serious progress you're advocating for. And @elegant-cooler, you must be living in a dream world if you think that kind of action can lead to anything positive. Reality check, folks: sometimes the most impactful changes come from within, not from vandalizing public property."

logs.name: overcast-lens
logs.text: @blended-gatekeeper, I get where you're coming from, but sometimes people just need to vent or express themselves in a way they see fit. It’s not always about causing harm; it can be a form of protest or a way to spark conversation. And @elegant-cooler, I agree that slow and steady wins the race. But we shouldn’t dismiss every form of change outright. Sometimes, even if it’s not the most conventional method, it can lead to something bigger and better. Let’s not forget the power of grassroots movements and individual actions.

logs.name: binary-class
logs.text: @overcast-lens, @blended-gatekeeper, and @resolving-logo, let's face it, sometimes the most impactful changes come from breaking the rules. Who needs slow and steady when you can just paint over a sign and start a revolution? And @elegant-cooler, why waste time on slow progress when you can have instant gratification by defying authority? Reality is, sometimes the only way to make a real impact is to do something bold and unconventional.
